sentences.text: My name is Eva Safferman.
Zysman, Z-Y-S-M-A-N.
I live in Fair Lawn, New Jersey, 7 Sandow Court.
No, no, I don't.
In Lodz, Poland, L-O-D-Z, Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D.
Zachary.
I have to spell it?
No, no.
You would like it?
Z-A-C-H-A-R-Y.
My father was an accountant and a businessman.
Yes, he did.
I think it was some kind of clothing place, manufacturing plant.
I believe it was men's clothing.
Yeah, I think pants or something like this.
He service a very, very large company, which was doing the tar for the roofs in Poland.
It's one of the biggest company.
I think it was [?
Oppenheimer ?] was the name of the company.
I think the owners were Jewish.
I believe he was born in 1903.
I don't remember the month.
No, I was the only child.
Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I really don't remember that well.
I was kind of young.
But he used to take me out.
He used to bring me stuff, food, all the best things.
When they weren't available to everybody else, I used to have them.
Oh, this was already when the war broke out.
It was about 1939.
Well, we had a very nice apartment with a terrace.
It was in a building that my grandfather owned, which my father really financed.
And we had a very nice apartment-- two bedrooms and kitchen.
We were few of the lucky ones.
They had a bathroom in the house.
Not too many people in Lodz had it.
Poludnowa 94, P-O-L-U-D-N-O-W-A, 94.
My mother's name was Anna.
Before the war they called her [?
Hanaka, ?] [?
Hanaka ?] Zysman, born Berman.
Mother was born in, I believe, in December 1998.
She was a very pretty woman, dark hair, very nice.
She was a homemaker.
She was home.
She's taking care of the three of us.
Well, she had a maid, but she did the cooking.
Well, for Saturday, she would make gefilte fish.
She would cook, you know, chicken soup, traditional Jewish food.
Right.
She was--
No.
She used to come in in the morning and leave at night.
Oh, I don't remember.
I think it was [?
Marisa. ?] I'm not sure, but I believe so.
I remember-- yeah.
She used to tell me stories and used to sing to me.
And then I used to get scared sometimes because she used to tell me those scary stories.
Oh, at that time I was maybe eight.
Yes.
She was-- she liked to embroider.
She liked to make beautiful pictures.
The house was full of embroidered stuff that she made-- pictures, tablecloth.
She was very, very talented this way.
By hand.
I really don't recall.
But sometimes they-- you buy already made, and then she would just embroider them.
My grandparents were very, very religious.
My father was not as religious.
And my mother, she-- she had to do whatever, you know, her husband.
She-- she had a kosher home, but she-- she wasn't religious.
My father was the one that was a little bit lax, because sometimes he had to go into work on a Saturday.
And of course, I was very heartbroken because I didn't like that.
All my friends were very religious.
Sometimes I used to cry because my father turned on the light on Saturday.
Yeah.
I had friends, children that lived in the same house, same building.
We used to play together.
I used to have a bicycle.
I used to let them ride on my bicycle, because this was kind of luxury before the war.
Yes.
It was a large industrial city.
Like New York is in the United States, that was Lodz was in Poland.
Yes, I did.
I went to public school on [NON-ENGLISH] 18.
And then the war broke out.
I believe I was in the sixth grade, I-- because I think I skipped.
I-- I don't remember really.
Arithmetic, Polish, spelling, geography.
Was-- was kind of long time ago.
Not in Poland, no, afterwards.
Well, my mother took a rebbetzin for me to give me some instructions how to read, but I wasn't too good in it.
I used to memorize one story.
Because the rebbetzin used to tell-- give me a-- to read a-- a love story she had in the back of her book, and I learned one by heart.
And every time she asked me, and I wanted to read this one, till she got wise to me.
She says, read something else, and I couldn't.
Yes, yes.
I used to go with my mom in a spa before the war.
We went to [?
Sczcawnica, ?] which was a-- a resort in Poland.
In the-- in the mountains.
Yeah.
No, no, it was in the mountains.
It was beautiful there.
I was a little girl.
I remember they used to have, like, a goat, and they used to milk the goat and I would drink the goat milk.
It was really nice.
Oh, this was-- I was a very poor eater and, like, always so green.
I didn't want to eat.
So my mom decided that she send me away with my aunt, which was my Aunt [?
Manucia, ?] to a camp so I should learn how to eat.
And I did go to the camp the-- the same year that the war broke out.
I did learn how to eat.
But unfortunately, when I got back there wasn't too much to eat.
Well, no, just being with other children and seeing that they eat, and you know, it was different.
At home I was by myself, and I-- you know.
Please, swallow-- my mother used to run after me with a spoon.
But over there with other children I just really learned how to eat, and I guess I was getting older, too.
Yeah, yeah.
I had dresses.
I had patent leather shoes.
Yeah.
No, no.
Honestly, I don't.
Well, there was-- first of all, we had to put on a Jewish star.
We weren't supposed to be in the street after a certain hour.
We were not supposed to go all around, you know?
We just had to be stayed wherever we were.
This was still in Lodz, not in the ghetto yet.
But of course, already things were getting tough.
You couldn't get food as much as you-- you wanted to.
And my father used to be a good provider, but still there wasn't too much to be gotten, so.
Well, we heard it.
I mean, people were talking, and my father used to read the paper, and we had the radio.
And you heard what happened, because the-- the war broke out, and in three days we were already occupied by the Germans.
No, he was not.
This was against the law.
If they would found it, they would have probably executed him or punished him in some way.
Well, my mom already took all the jewelry and started hiding it in the stove.
He-- she took out a brick from the stove and took all her jewelry, because she had quite a lot that my father gave her, and she used to hide it.
Every time, you know, she heard somebody might be coming, she used to hide it away.
So it was kind of very unsettling.
Oh, yeah.
She had-- she had a diamond ring.
She had a beautiful diamond watch that he bought her once for her anniversary.
And he even bought a diamond ring for me.
He says I should have that.
Well--
The German people.
They used to come to the houses, and they used to inspect the houses.
And then one day, we-- we heard a big commotion outside, and there was a lot of Germans.
They came into the house.
And my mother heard it too.
They went to my grandfather's house, and they took everybody out-- my grandmother, my grandfather, all the aunts and uncles-- and they shipped them out.
So when my mother saw that, she went into the house, and she started packing all this-- a little stuff that we had.
She put some in valises.
And we were staying by the door when the German officer came in, and he asked my mother, what are you doing here?
So she says, well, I see that you taken away my-- my in-laws and all my brother and sister-in-laws.
Then I'm ready to go too.
So he comes over and looks at me and then says, oh, boy, is she Jewish?
And my mother says, yes, she's Jewish.
So he says, you go back.
You're not going anyplace.
So then we still-- we-- we remained in our apartment.
But all my aunt-- my-- all my aunts and uncles and my grandmother and grandfather was evacuated.
Did you ever see them again?
And we never-- no.
We heard that they were taken to [NON-ENGLISH], and I think they were executed there.
I guess because I didn't look Jewish.
I had blue eyes, and I had red hair.
Well, next we just unpacked everything again.
My mother, before we would-- ready to-- she left the window open because my father wasn't home, and so this way he could come back and take out the stuff that she hid in the stove.
And then so we-- we went back in, and we just stood on.
No.
At that time, no.
No, I didn't have any cousins.
I had one cousin, which this cousin left for the United States before the war.
And of course, other cousins, there were some were in Israel and some were in a different town.
They did not live-- all of them did not live in-- in Lodz.
The next time, there was-- we were supposed to leave our home, and we were supposed to go into the ghetto.
So that time, my father came back, and we packed everything, whatever little we could, and we marched with everybody else into the ghetto.
Oh, he was doing business.
He was still doing business during the war.
He would take off the Jewish star, and he would go and do-- conduct business.
It was a yellow-- like a Magen David, like a Jewish star.
And inside there was written in black, Jude, what means Jew.
Yeah.
You-- you-- you bought them, yes.
Yeah.
No, I can't remember now.
I really don't remember that much.
Yeah.
And we had to sew it on ourselves.
So then we all marched to the ghetto, and my father got us an apartment.
Yeah, we took a lot of things with us.
We couldn't take too much.
Some clothes, some bedding, a couple of pots, I guess, pans.
No, I don't remember, but maybe we did.
I don't remember that.
Well, other people marched too.
Everybody marched toward the ghetto.
And there was--
No we just walked by ourselves.
It was a part of the Lodz that I was never before there.
They-- this part was like poor part of Lodz.
So we went there.
We got an apartment.
It was one big room and a kitchen.
And I had a grandmother, which was in a nursing home at that time in Poland.
So my mother took the-- her mother out and she lived with us.
And of course, was the great hunger.
We had very little to eat.
There was rationed-- everything was rationed, like would get one ounce of kohlrabi and-- and maybe bread for a whole family for the whole week.
And-- but she stayed with us, till one day my father brought some horse meat that he got, because he worked for the-- for the-- he was an accountant in the ghetto [NON-ENGLISH].
So he was able to get this.
And when she saw this and she said, my dear children, I can't stay with you anymore, because I cannot stay and eat this non-kosher food.
And the nursing home was still in existence, so my father had to take her back.
Why?
Because my grandfather, when he passed on, he said to her, I don't want you to be-- you have eight children, but I never want you to be with any of them.
And he felt that she would be better off being in a nursing home.
And she was very happy there.
I remember even before the war, I used to-- right into the war-- in the war in the beginning, I used to bring her some food every Saturday, and I used to talk to all the ladies.
They used to send-- you know, save for me chestnuts, because they liked me.
I always used to come like the Little Red Riding Hood with the-- with the food for her grandmother.
Oh, grandma was about 89 or so, 90, like this.
Well, she was very lucky, because grandmother died a natural death about a week, or maybe it was a couple of weeks, before they-- the German came to the nursing home.
They murdered all the-- all the people in the nursing home.
Yeah.
No.
I don't-- I-- I didn't go.
I-- I guess in Poland there was a custom that if you have your parents you weren't supposed to go to a funeral.
It's a Jewish custom.
When you have living parents you're not supposed to go to the cemetery.
Even when they say the holy prayer, when there was Yizkor, you used to-- you have to get out, because-- if you had living parents.
Now, they don't believe this anymore.
They tell you you can stay on even there is the [INAUDIBLE], the prayers.
Yes, yeah.
She-- she did.
Well, this was in the ghetto.
This was-- the-- the-- the cemetery was in the ghetto.
Was in a part-- was called, I think, [NON-ENGLISH].
I-- I'm not sure.
But it was in the ghetto.
Yes, it was, yeah.
They did.
Yeah.
But she didn't stay too long there because she passed on, which was really lucky for her.
She-- at least we knew where she was buried.
Me?
I went to school, yes.
I went to school for a short while.
And then they just closed all the schools, and everybody had to go to work.
So they send me to a place.
They were making carpets.
It was a [NON-ENGLISH] resort.
It consisted-- we had like a frame with some kind of a cotton going through.
And we used to get pieces of material and make long, like, long pieces of material, and then we used to weave it in and make designs.
They really came out quite nice.
The only thing, sometimes we got stuff from people that were evacuated, and we saw there, like, three shirts one into the other.
Because a lot of people, when they were shipped out of the ghetto, would wear a couple of things, you know, one on top of the other.
Right, some of the clothing was bloodstained.
The time in the ghetto was the worst part of-- of our lives, because we lived in constant fear.
We never knew when there's going to be an appell and when they're going to take people off the streets.
You were never sure where you're going to be when they're going to catch you.
And that's what happened to my father.
He was once walking in the streets, and they caught him on a truck, and they-- with a lot of other people, and he had no chance.
And they just evacuated them and shipped them.
We never heard of them again.
No, we don't.
We don't know.
He was gone, and this was the last I ever heard of him.
It was me and my mother that was left.
We stood there for a little while, and then they decided they have to concentrate people in-- in smaller places because they need more room.
And they put me and my mother into another-- couple with a daughter, in their apartment.
So they gave us a bed on the side.
And they hated us, like it was our fault that they put us into their houses.
But it really, you know, was not our fault.
We didn't want to leave our house.
The Germans, yeah.
They-- this was really the Jewish government on the instruction of the Germans, because they told them what to do.
Right, yes.
There was a government.
There was policemen.
There was-- there was, you know, a whole body.
Like, the president was Rumkowski, and he has a lot of people.
Like, my father worked for them, too, before they caught him.
And no matter what he said, they just-- you couldn't talk to anybody.
They just put you in a truck and just evacuated you.
They were the people that were issuing all kinds of rules that we had to obey, even though the-- it was just the German that told the Jews what to do.
So we had to go to the other people's apartment.
I really can't recall.
But it--
And a daughter.
And they were very mean to us.
They put us-- our bed on the place where there was-- the water was coming down when it rains.
And once my mother and me had to sleep with an umbrella, holding the umbrella up.
And it was really very tough times.

Because every time that the people came, that we heard that the Germans are coming to look for people, we used to hide in that little-- there was a little tiny-- it was like a little house but with one room, more like a storage room.
Then me and my mom used to hide when we heard that the Germans were coming.
So we were laying underneath a headboard under the window.
So when the Germans would look in, they wouldn't see us.
So that's how we were hiding, you know.
I don't remember that, because we left before that.
Well, as I told you, I was-- I was working in the carpet factory.

And then after a short while, they disbanded it and sent all the young children to a other factory, in a factory that were makings, I think, believe, was uniforms for the Germans, and we worked there.
And from there, there was-- the German ask the director of this factory occasionally to give him names, people they should evacuate.
And one day, there was a list, and my name was on this list.
And I knew that I have to hide unless they come and-- and look for me.
I had an uncle which was a policeman in the ghetto.
So when I saw the name, I went to him and I asked him, please, could you do something?
You know, the director of the place, they should take off my name from the list.
And he did, and he managed to take off my name.
But unfortunately for me, my name was typed twice, and my-- the other name was still on this list.
And one day they came to look for me.
I was on-- we were in this little place that we were.
This was a-- a couple of apartment were spread out, and we had a little bit of ground in the back.
And all the tenants used to divide a little piece of ground, and we used to put some-- grow some vegetables-- radishes, beets, cucumbers.
And we also had three fruit trees, which really helped us in the ghetto.
But so we used to divide whatever fruit would grow against the tenants, and everybody got a little bit.
And one day when I was in the back there, I heard somebody come in and yell, Eva Zysman.
And I had a bad feeling about it because I knew my name was on the list.
So I ran in the back, and I lay down between the-- the beet-- the beets, and I covered myself with the beets.
Oh, yes.
I put it all around.
And they came to my mother and ask her, where is Eva?
And she says, I don't know.
I don't know where she is.
So they said, well, we're going to wait.
Says, I don't know how long it's going to take when she comes.
But she says, here-- she gave him a bag of apples.
Please write down that she's not here.
So OK, they took the apples.
They went away.
And this was the first time that-- to save me.
